title: 32M — Agitated Male at Gas Station
dispatch: 32-year-old male, extremely agitated, violent behavior, possible drug use
scene: You arrive at a gas station parking lot with law enforcement already on scene. A 32-year-old male is pacing rapidly, sweating profusely, and shouting incoherently. He has removed his shirt despite 40°F weather. Store employees report he was breaking items inside and exhibited superhuman strength when two officers attempted to restrain him. He appears to have no awareness of pain, as evidenced by bleeding lacerations on his hands from punching a glass display case. His skin is hot and diaphoretic.

history.pmh: Unknown — patient unable to provide history

questions.prompt: This patient is displaying signs of a time-sensitive, life-threatening condition. Select ALL clinical findings that support this assessment.

options.text: Hyperthermia with a temperature of 104.2°F
options.explanation: Severe hyperthermia in the setting of extreme agitation indicates a hypermetabolic state that can rapidly progress to multi-organ failure, rhabdomyolysis, and cardiac arrest if not aggressively managed.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Excited Delirium; National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Extreme agitation with apparent insensitivity to pain
options.explanation: Pain insensitivity combined with extreme agitation is a hallmark of excited delirium syndrome and indicates significant CNS dysfunction with high risk of sudden cardiac arrest.
options.guideline_ref: ACEP White Paper on Excited Delirium 2009; National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Tachycardia at 162 beats per minute
options.explanation: Severe tachycardia reflects the hypermetabolic and hypercatecholamine state. Sustained tachycardia at this rate increases myocardial oxygen demand and risk of cardiac dysrhythmias.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Diaphoresis and undressing despite cold ambient temperature
options.explanation: Paradoxical undressing in cold weather with profuse diaphoresis indicates severe thermoregulatory dysfunction, a key feature of excited delirium syndrome.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Excited Delirium

options.text: Bleeding lacerations on the patient's hands from punching glass
options.explanation: While the lacerations require attention, they are a consequence of the agitated behavior rather than a distinguishing indicator of the underlying life-threatening condition. The systemic findings are more diagnostically significant.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

questions.clinical_pearl: Excited delirium is a medical emergency, not simply a behavioral problem. These patients are at high risk for sudden cardiac arrest. Rapid sedation, cooling, and transport are priorities — prolonged physical restraint increases metabolic demand and death risk.

questions.prompt: Arrange the following interventions in the correct order of priority for this patient.
items: Administer ketamine 4 mg/kg IM for rapid chemical sedation
Coordinate with law enforcement for safe patient access while preparing sedation
Apply continuous cardiac monitoring and assess for dysrhythmias
Initiate active cooling measures including cold IV fluids and exposure
Establish IV/IO access and prepare for potential cardiac arrest
questions.explanation: First, coordinate a safe approach with law enforcement while drawing up sedation — crew safety is always the priority. Second, administer chemical sedation as rapidly as possible to reduce the hypermetabolic state. Third, apply cardiac monitoring as these patients are at extreme risk for lethal dysrhythmias. Fourth, establish vascular access. Fifth, initiate cooling measures. Chemical sedation is the cornerstone of treatment — it reduces metabolic demand, lowers temperature, and decreases cardiac arrest risk.
questions.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Excited Delirium; National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies
questions.clinical_pearl: Ketamine is preferred in excited delirium because it provides rapid, reliable sedation via IM route without requiring IV access, has a favorable hemodynamic profile, and is less likely to cause respiratory depression than benzodiazepines at sedation doses.

questions.prompt: After chemical sedation, what is the MOST important positional consideration for this patient?
questions.narrative: The patient has been chemically sedated and is no longer combative. He is now obtunded with sonorous respirations. Restraints remain in place.

options.text: Place in a lateral recumbent position and continuously monitor airway and respirations
options.explanation: Once sedated, the patient must be immediately repositioned from prone to a lateral recumbent or supine position with continuous airway monitoring. Prone positioning with restraints significantly increases the risk of positional asphyxia and death.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint; National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Keep the patient prone with hands restrained behind the back to prevent re-agitation
options.explanation: Prone positioning with hands behind the back is associated with positional asphyxia and sudden death, especially after chemical sedation when the patient cannot protect their own airway. This position must be avoided.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint

options.text: Sit the patient upright in a stair chair to maximize respiratory effort during transport
options.explanation: An obtunded patient with a GCS of 8 should not be placed upright in a stair chair due to aspiration risk and inability to maintain their own airway. Lateral positioning is preferred.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Airway Management

options.text: Place supine with bilateral soft wrist restraints secured to opposite stretcher rails
options.explanation: While supine with restraints is acceptable for an alert patient, this obtunded patient with sonorous respirations needs lateral positioning to protect the airway. Supine positioning increases aspiration risk in patients with altered mental status.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint

questions.clinical_pearl: Any restrained patient who becomes sedated or loses consciousness must be immediately reassessed and repositioned. Positional asphyxia can occur in minutes. Never leave a sedated patient prone or hobble-restrained.

title: 19M — Behavioral Crisis at Group Home
dispatch: 19-year-old male, behavioral emergency, staff requesting assistance
scene: You respond to a group home for adults with developmental disabilities. Staff reports that a 19-year-old male resident with autism spectrum disorder became severely agitated after an unexpected fire alarm test 30 minutes ago. He is in his room, rocking back and forth, repeatedly hitting his head against the wall, and screaming. Staff attempted to calm him but physical touch made him more agitated. He is wearing noise-canceling headphones that were knocked off during the episode. The room lights are fluorescent and flickering. A caregiver provides his communication board and medical binder.

history.pmh: Autism spectrum disorder (level 2 support needs), sensory processing disorder, epilepsy (controlled)
history.meds: Levetiracetam 500 mg BID, risperidone 1 mg BID

questions.prompt: Categorize each of the following approaches as either 'Appropriate' or 'Inappropriate' for this patient during initial contact.
items: Reduce sensory stimulation by turning off fluorescent lights and silencing radio chatter
Firmly grab the patient's arms to prevent further self-injury
Use the communication board provided by staff to attempt interaction
Introduce multiple crew members simultaneously to establish rapport quickly
Allow the patient space and time; approach slowly with predictable movements
Offer the patient his noise-canceling headphones
questions.explanation: Reducing sensory input (lights, noise), using familiar communication tools, allowing space, and restoring comfort items (headphones) are all appropriate strategies for autistic patients in crisis. Unexpected physical contact and overwhelming the patient with multiple new people will escalate agitation. Patients with ASD are often hypersensitive to sensory stimuli, and crisis management focuses on reducing environmental triggers.
questions.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Patients with Special Challenges; NAEMSP Guidelines on Special Populations
questions.clinical_pearl: Autism is not a behavioral disorder — it is a neurological difference. Sensory overload is a common trigger for crisis. The EMS approach should prioritize environmental modification before pharmacological intervention.

questions.prompt: After 10 minutes of environmental modifications, the patient has stopped hitting his head but remains highly distressed and is now hyperventilating. Staff reports he has a PRN medication in his care plan for crisis situations. As an EMT, what is your MOST appropriate next step?
questions.narrative: The flickering light has been turned off and replaced with a flashlight pointed at the ceiling. The patient accepted his headphones. He is sitting on the floor, rocking, and hyperventilating but no longer self-injuring.

options.text: Assist the patient in taking his prescribed PRN medication per his care plan with staff assistance
options.explanation: Many patients with ASD in residential settings have individualized crisis plans that include PRN medications. Facilitating administration of prescribed medications with caregiver assistance is appropriate and within EMT scope when the patient has the medication prescribed and available.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Patients with Special Challenges

options.text: Physically restrain the patient to the stretcher for immediate transport to the emergency department
options.explanation: The patient is improving with environmental modifications and is no longer self-injuring. Physical restraint in an ASD patient is likely to dramatically escalate the crisis and should only be used when there is an immediate threat to life.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Coach the patient through breathing exercises using visual hand gestures to manage the hyperventilation
options.explanation: While coaching breathing can be helpful, introducing new abstract concepts during an active crisis may not be effective for this patient. The PRN medication from his established care plan is a more reliable intervention.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Patients with Special Challenges

options.text: Request ALS intercept for chemical sedation due to the patient's continued agitation
options.explanation: The patient is improving — he stopped self-injury and accepted his headphones. Chemical sedation by ALS is not indicated when de-escalation is working and the patient has a care plan with PRN medications available.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

questions.clinical_pearl: Always ask caregivers of patients with developmental disabilities about individualized care plans, crisis protocols, preferred communication methods, and sensory triggers. Caregivers are your best resource and should be included in the care team.

questions.prompt: The patient has calmed significantly and staff requests transport for evaluation of the head-banging injury. Which considerations are MOST important for transporting this patient?

options.text: Allow a familiar caregiver to ride in the ambulance with the patient
options.explanation: A familiar face provides comfort and can assist with communication and de-escalation during transport. This is particularly important for patients with ASD who may become re-agitated in the unfamiliar ambulance environment.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Patients with Special Challenges

options.text: Minimize sirens and unnecessary lights during transport
options.explanation: Auditory and visual stimulation from sirens and lights can re-trigger sensory overload in ASD patients. Non-emergent transport with reduced stimulation is appropriate given the patient's current stability.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Patients with Special Challenges

options.text: Bring the patient's communication board and comfort items
options.explanation: Communication tools and comfort items are essential for continuity of care. Without them, the patient may be unable to communicate needs or distress at the hospital, potentially leading to a recurrence of the crisis.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Patients with Special Challenges

options.text: Apply bilateral soft wrist restraints prophylactically in case of re-agitation
options.explanation: Prophylactic restraints are not indicated for a patient who is currently calm and cooperative. Restraints should only be applied when there is an active, immediate threat. Prophylactic restraints in ASD patients often cause escalation.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint

options.text: Administer supplemental oxygen via non-rebreather mask to prevent hypoxia during transport
options.explanation: The patient's SpO2 is 99% and there is no clinical indication for supplemental oxygen. Placing a mask on the face of a patient with sensory processing disorder is likely to cause distress and re-escalation.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Airway Management

questions.clinical_pearl: For patients with ASD, the ambulance itself can be a sensory nightmare — confined space, unfamiliar smells, vibrations, and bright lights. Pre-planning the transport environment can prevent a secondary crisis.

title: 81F — Confused Elderly Female, Welfare Check
dispatch: 81-year-old female, welfare check requested by neighbor, possible neglect
scene: You respond to a welfare check at a single-family home. A neighbor called stating she hasn't seen the resident in two weeks and noticed a foul odor. Law enforcement has made entry. Inside, you find an 81-year-old female seated in a recliner in a darkened living room. The home is in severe disrepair with spoiled food on the counter, no running water, and feces on the floor. The patient is emaciated, wearing soiled clothing, and has a large stage III pressure ulcer on her sacrum visible when she shifts position. Her live-in son is present and becomes hostile when questioned, stating 'She's fine, she doesn't want to go to the hospital.' The patient appears frightened and avoids eye contact with her son.

history.meds: Unknown — multiple empty pill bottles on the counter with refill dates months overdue

questions.prompt: Select ALL findings at this scene that are indicators of elder abuse or neglect.

options.text: Stage III pressure ulcer on the sacrum indicating prolonged immobility without repositioning
options.explanation: A stage III pressure ulcer in a patient with a caregiver present indicates neglect — the caregiver has failed to reposition the patient, provide wound care, or seek medical attention for an advanced wound.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect; Elder Justice Act

options.text: Severely emaciated appearance with evidence of malnutrition and dehydration
options.explanation: Malnutrition and dehydration in a dependent adult with a live-in caregiver is a hallmark of neglect. The caregiver has a duty to ensure basic nutritional needs are met.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

options.text: Multiple overdue medication refills suggesting medical neglect
options.explanation: Failure to maintain prescribed medications for a dependent adult with dementia constitutes medical neglect. The empty, unrefilled bottles indicate the caregiver has not ensured continuity of medical care.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

options.text: Unsanitary living conditions with no running water and spoiled food
options.explanation: Deplorable living conditions in the home of a dependent adult with an identified caregiver represent environmental neglect.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

options.text: The patient appears frightened and avoids eye contact with her son
options.explanation: Fearful behavior toward a caregiver is a behavioral indicator of abuse. The patient may be afraid of repercussions for disclosing mistreatment.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

options.text: The patient's age of 81 years and diagnosis of dementia
options.explanation: While advanced age and dementia are risk factors for abuse and neglect, they are not themselves indicators. Many elderly patients with dementia receive excellent care. The indicators are the objective findings of neglect in the presence of a responsible caregiver.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

questions.clinical_pearl: Document findings objectively and thoroughly — your documentation may be the first medical record in an elder abuse investigation. Note the environment, the patient's condition, caregiver behavior, and any statements made, using direct quotes when possible.

questions.prompt: The patient's son refuses to allow transport, stating he has power of attorney. The patient whispers 'Please help me' when her son steps out of the room. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.text: Coordinate with law enforcement on scene to ensure the patient is transported; report suspected abuse per mandatory reporting requirements
options.explanation: EMS providers are mandatory reporters of suspected abuse and neglect in all 50 states. The patient has expressed a desire for help, there is clear evidence of neglect, and the patient's medical condition requires treatment. Law enforcement can intervene to ensure patient safety. Power of attorney does not authorize a caregiver to deny emergency medical care to a patient who is requesting help.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect; Mandatory Reporting Laws

options.text: Honor the son's power of attorney and leave the patient at home with a refusal form
options.explanation: Power of attorney does not override a patient's own expressed wishes when the patient demonstrates capacity to request help. Additionally, EMS providers cannot leave a patient in a clearly unsafe environment with evidence of active neglect and abuse.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Consent and Refusal; Abuse and Neglect

options.text: Contact medical control for permission to transport against the son's wishes
options.explanation: While contacting medical control can be helpful, it is not required to transport a patient who is requesting help. The patient herself has expressed a desire for assistance, and law enforcement is already on scene to manage the son's objections.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

options.text: Provide treatment on scene but leave the patient at home and file a report with Adult Protective Services later
options.explanation: Treating and leaving the patient in this environment exposes her to continued harm. The patient has a stage III pressure ulcer, is hypothermic, hypotensive, and dehydrated — she requires emergency department evaluation. Leaving her would be unconscionable.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Abuse and Neglect

questions.clinical_pearl: Power of attorney grants authority to make decisions when a patient cannot. It does not give a caregiver the right to deny care to a patient who is expressing their own wishes. When there is suspected abuse, the patient's safety always comes first.

questions.prompt: During transport, you assess the patient more thoroughly. Arrange the following treatment priorities in order.
items: Passive rewarming with blankets and warmed ambulance compartment
Establish IV access and begin warm normal saline infusion at 250 mL/hr
Cover the sacral pressure ulcer with a sterile moist dressing
Perform a blood glucose check given her diabetic history and missed medications
Continuous cardiac monitoring given hypothermia and electrolyte concerns
questions.explanation: First, check blood glucose — hypoglycemia in a diabetic who has been neglected and is not eating is an immediately life-threatening and easily treatable condition. Second, begin passive rewarming as hypothermia contributes to hemodynamic instability. Third, apply cardiac monitoring as hypothermic patients are prone to dysrhythmias. Fourth, establish IV access and administer warm fluids for dehydration and continued rewarming (AEMT/Paramedic). Fifth, dress the wound — while important, this is less time-critical than the metabolic and thermoregulatory concerns.
questions.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Geriatric Emergencies; Environmental Emergencies
questions.clinical_pearl: Elderly neglect patients often present with a cascade of problems: dehydration, malnutrition, hypothermia, infection, and medication non-compliance. Treat the most immediately lethal conditions first while being vigilant for sepsis from the infected pressure ulcer.

title: 38F — Anxious Female After Traffic Accident
dispatch: 38-year-old female, panic attack, bystander at minor MVA scene
scene: You are on scene at a minor fender-bender on a residential street. A bystander flags you down and says a woman sitting on the curb is 'freaking out.' The 38-year-old female was not involved in the crash but was walking nearby when it occurred. She is trembling uncontrollably, hyperventilating, and staring blankly. When you approach, she flinches and says 'I can't be here, the explosion — I need to get out.' She then becomes oriented and tearfully explains she is an Army veteran and the sound of the crash triggered memories. She is clutching her purse tightly and appears terrified but is not aggressive.

history.pmh: PTSD (combat-related), generalized anxiety disorder, chronic insomnia
history.meds: Sertraline 100 mg daily, prazosin 2 mg at bedtime, alprazolam 0.5 mg PRN

questions.prompt: What is the MOST appropriate initial approach when making contact with this patient?

options.text: Approach from the front at eye level, identify yourself calmly, ask permission before any physical contact, and offer to move to a quieter location
options.explanation: Patients experiencing PTSD-triggered crisis may be hypervigilant and easily startled. Approaching from the front prevents triggering a startle response, asking permission respects autonomy and establishes trust, and moving away from the triggering stimulus (crash scene) reduces ongoing stimulation.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; SAMHSA Trauma-Informed Care Guidelines

options.text: Quickly assess her vital signs and perform a rapid physical exam to rule out injuries from the accident
options.explanation: The patient was not involved in the accident. Rushing to perform physical assessment without establishing rapport could be perceived as threatening and worsen the crisis. A trauma-informed approach prioritizes psychological safety before physical assessment.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Place a hand on her shoulder reassuringly while telling her she is safe and the crash is over
options.explanation: Unsolicited physical contact with a patient experiencing a PTSD flashback can be perceived as a threat and may trigger a fight-or-flight response or escalate distress. Always ask before touching.
options.guideline_ref: SAMHSA Trauma-Informed Care Guidelines

options.text: Ask detailed questions about her military service to help her process the triggering event
options.explanation: Asking about traumatic experiences during an acute crisis is inappropriate and can worsen symptoms. The goal is stabilization and grounding, not trauma processing. That is the role of mental health professionals in a therapeutic setting.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

questions.clinical_pearl: Trauma-informed care means recognizing that many patients have experienced trauma, and our standard EMS practices (uniforms, confined spaces, loss of control) can be re-traumatizing. Simple adjustments — asking permission, explaining actions, providing choices — make a significant difference.

questions.prompt: You have moved the patient to the back of the ambulance, away from the crash scene. She is still hyperventilating and trembling but is oriented and cooperative. Categorize each intervention as either 'Appropriate for This Patient' or 'Not Appropriate for This Patient.'
items: Guide grounding techniques: ask her to name 5 things she can see, 4 she can touch
Administer high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM
Offer to assist her with her prescribed PRN alprazolam if she has it with her
Use calm, steady voice to coach slow breathing techniques
Apply a paper bag over her mouth and nose to correct hyperventilation
questions.explanation: Grounding techniques (5-4-3-2-1 method) are evidence-based interventions for acute PTSD episodes. Coaching breathing is effective and non-invasive. Assisting with a prescribed PRN anxiolytic is appropriate. High-flow oxygen is unnecessary with SpO2 of 99% and may increase anxiety (mask on face). Paper bag rebreathing is dangerous and outdated — it can cause hypoxia and is contraindicated.
questions.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; SAMHSA Guidelines
questions.clinical_pearl: The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique helps patients with PTSD reconnect with the present moment: name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste. It interrupts the dissociative flashback cycle.

questions.prompt: After 15 minutes, the patient's symptoms have largely resolved. She is calm, oriented, and her vitals have normalized. She thanks you and states she does not want to go to the hospital. What is the MOST appropriate disposition?

options.text: The patient has capacity to refuse; obtain informed refusal, encourage follow-up with her mental health provider, and provide crisis hotline information
options.explanation: This patient is alert, oriented, has capacity, and her acute crisis has resolved with normal vitals. She has the right to refuse transport. Providing mental health follow-up resources and crisis line information is an important part of the disposition.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Consent and Refusal; Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Transport the patient involuntarily because PTSD flashbacks indicate she is a danger to herself
options.explanation: A PTSD flashback that has resolved does not meet criteria for involuntary commitment. The patient is oriented, calm, has capacity, and is not expressing suicidal or homicidal ideation. Involuntary transport is not warranted.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; Involuntary Commitment Criteria

options.text: Leave the scene without documentation since no medical treatment was provided
options.explanation: All patient contacts require documentation, including refusals. The assessment, interventions (grounding, de-escalation), and informed refusal must be documented.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Documentation

options.text: Contact law enforcement to place the patient on an involuntary psychiatric hold
options.explanation: The patient's crisis has resolved and she does not meet criteria for involuntary commitment (imminent danger to self or others, or gravely disabled). Law enforcement involvement is not indicated.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

questions.clinical_pearl: Veterans with PTSD who experience acute crisis episodes often have established mental health care and coping strategies. Connecting them with the Veterans Crisis Line (988, press 1) and encouraging follow-up with their existing provider is more valuable than a potentially re-traumatizing ED visit.

title: 54M — Confused Male, Tremors, Found by Family
dispatch: 54-year-old male, confusion, shaking, family requesting assistance
scene: You arrive at an apartment where the patient's sister called 911. A 54-year-old male is sitting on the couch, visibly tremorous with coarse bilateral hand tremors. He is diaphoretic and keeps swatting at things that aren't there, yelling 'Get them off me!' His sister states he is a heavy daily drinker and was admitted to the hospital last week for pancreatitis. He was discharged 3 days ago and she believes he has not had any alcohol since admission — roughly 10 days total. She found multiple empty liquor bottles during a visit yesterday but he insists he hasn't been drinking. He had a witnessed seizure approximately 2 hours ago lasting about 90 seconds.

history.pmh: Alcohol use disorder (severe), chronic pancreatitis, alcoholic hepatitis, prior alcohol withdrawal seizure (2 years ago)
history.meds: Prescribed thiamine and folate at discharge — bottles unopened

questions.prompt: Based on the clinical presentation, arrange the following assessment findings from MOST concerning to LEAST concerning in terms of immediate life threat.
items: History of witnessed seizure 2 hours ago
Visual hallucinations with agitation
Heart rate of 128 with blood pressure 168/104
Temperature of 101.8°F
Coarse bilateral tremors
questions.explanation: The seizure history is most concerning because it indicates severe withdrawal with risk of recurrent seizures and status epilepticus. Visual hallucinations with agitation indicate delirium tremens, which carries 5-15% mortality without treatment. Fever suggests significant autonomic dysfunction and possible concurrent infection. Tachycardia and hypertension reflect autonomic hyperactivity. Tremors, while characteristic, are the least immediately life-threatening finding.
questions.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse; CIWA-Ar Assessment Scale
questions.clinical_pearl: Alcohol withdrawal exists on a spectrum: mild (6-12h: tremors, anxiety), moderate (12-48h: hallucinations, seizures), and severe/DTs (48-96h: delirium, autonomic instability, hyperthermia). DTs represent the most dangerous phase with significant mortality if untreated.

questions.prompt: As a Paramedic, what is the MOST appropriate pharmacological intervention for this patient?

options.text: Midazolam 5 mg IM or IV with repeat dosing as needed to control agitation and prevent seizures
options.explanation: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens. They address the underlying GABA deficit, control agitation, prevent seizures, and reduce autonomic hyperactivity. Midazolam IM provides rapid onset when IV access is difficult in agitated patients.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse; ASAM Guidelines for Alcohol Withdrawal Management

options.text: Haloperidol 5 mg IM for management of hallucinations and psychotic symptoms
options.explanation: Haloperidol and other antipsychotics lower the seizure threshold and do not address the underlying pathophysiology of alcohol withdrawal. In DTs, antipsychotics can precipitate seizures and worsen the clinical course. Benzodiazepines are the appropriate first-line treatment.
options.guideline_ref: ASAM Guidelines for Alcohol Withdrawal Management

options.text: Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM for rapid sedation of the agitated patient
options.explanation: While ketamine is useful for excited delirium and acute agitation from other causes, benzodiazepines are specifically indicated for alcohol withdrawal because they directly address the GABA-receptor deficit that causes the withdrawal syndrome.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

options.text: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV for sedation and control of agitation
options.explanation: Diphenhydramine is not appropriate for alcohol withdrawal management. It has anticholinergic properties that can worsen delirium and does not address the underlying pathophysiology or seizure risk.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

questions.clinical_pearl: Patients in DTs often require very large doses of benzodiazepines — far more than typical dosing. Repeat dosing should target clinical effect: reduction in agitation, tremor, and autonomic hyperactivity. Under-dosing is a common and dangerous mistake.

questions.prompt: Select ALL additional interventions appropriate for this patient during transport.

options.text: Establish IV access and administer a normal saline fluid bolus for dehydration
options.explanation: Patients in alcohol withdrawal are typically significantly dehydrated from poor oral intake, diaphoresis, and vomiting. IV fluid resuscitation is important.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

options.text: Administer thiamine 100 mg IV before any dextrose-containing fluids
options.explanation: Chronic alcoholics are thiamine-depleted. Thiamine should be administered to prevent or treat Wernicke's encephalopathy. While the traditional teaching of giving thiamine before dextrose is debated, administering thiamine is essential.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

options.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring for dysrhythmia detection
options.explanation: Autonomic instability in DTs combined with electrolyte abnormalities (particularly hypomagnesemia and hypokalemia common in chronic alcoholics) creates significant dysrhythmia risk.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

options.text: Check blood glucose level
options.explanation: Chronic alcoholics are at high risk for hypoglycemia due to impaired hepatic gluconeogenesis and poor nutritional intake. Altered mental status in this population always warrants glucose assessment.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Endocrine Emergencies

options.text: Administer activated charcoal for possible alcohol ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal does not adsorb alcohol and is not indicated. Additionally, the patient's issue is withdrawal from alcohol, not acute intoxication.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology

options.text: Apply physical restraints to all four extremities for safety during transport
options.explanation: Chemical sedation with benzodiazepines should be the primary intervention to manage agitation in DTs. Physical restraints increase metabolic demand and can worsen hyperthermia. If needed, use the minimum restraint necessary after adequate sedation.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint

questions.clinical_pearl: The triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia suggests Wernicke's encephalopathy — but all three findings are present in less than 30% of cases. Administer thiamine to all chronic alcohol users with altered mental status, regardless of the full triad.

title: 27M — Agitated Male, Body Aches, Motel Room
dispatch: 27-year-old male, altered behavior, body aches, motel room

patient.cc: Severe body aches, agitation, nausea, anxiety

scene: You respond to a motel room where the manager called 911 after hearing screaming. A 27-year-old male is found sitting on the bathroom floor, agitated and restless. He is alternately pacing and lying in the fetal position. He is yawning excessively, has rhinorrhea, and his clothes are soaked with sweat. He states he ran out of his 'medicine' two days ago and 'can't take this anymore.' You notice pinpoint pupils, gooseflesh on his arms, and track marks on the antecubital fossae bilaterally. He is tearful and states he wants help but becomes irritable when touched. There is no drug paraphernalia visible.

history.pmh: Opioid use disorder, hepatitis C, depression
history.meds: Was prescribed buprenorphine/naloxone — ran out of prescription

questions.prompt: Categorize each finding as either a sign of 'Opioid Withdrawal' or 'Opioid Overdose.'
items: Pinpoint pupils
Hypertension (152/94)
Tachycardia (108 bpm)
Excessive yawning and rhinorrhea
Piloerection (gooseflesh)
Diaphoresis with restless agitation
questions.explanation: Pinpoint pupils (miosis) are associated with opioid overdose/intoxication — they are a parasympathetic effect of opioid use. All other findings are characteristic of withdrawal: sympathetic nervous system hyperactivation causes hypertension, tachycardia, and diaphoresis. Yawning, rhinorrhea, piloerection, and restlessness are classic withdrawal symptoms. Note: pinpoint pupils can transiently persist into early withdrawal but are classically an intoxication finding.
questions.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse; COWS (Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale)
questions.clinical_pearl: Opioid withdrawal, unlike alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal, is rarely life-threatening in otherwise healthy adults. However, it causes extreme subjective distress and is a major driver of relapse. Treat the patient with empathy and dignity.

questions.prompt: The patient asks you for help and agrees to transport. What is the MOST appropriate approach for this patient?

options.text: Provide supportive care including IV fluids for dehydration, ondansetron for nausea, and compassionate transport to an ED with addiction medicine resources
options.explanation: Opioid withdrawal management in the prehospital setting is primarily supportive. IV fluids address dehydration from diaphoresis and vomiting, antiemetics improve comfort, and transport to a facility with addiction medicine services facilitates continuity of care and potential re-initiation of medication-assisted treatment.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse; SAMHSA Treatment Guidelines

options.text: Administer naloxone 2 mg intranasal to manage the patient's opioid-related symptoms
options.explanation: Naloxone is an opioid antagonist used for opioid overdose. Administering naloxone to a patient in opioid withdrawal will dramatically worsen symptoms and could precipitate a severe withdrawal crisis. Naloxone is absolutely contraindicated in withdrawal.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

options.text: Refuse transport since opioid withdrawal is not a medical emergency and advise the patient to contact his prescriber
options.explanation: While opioid withdrawal is rarely life-threatening, the patient is requesting help and has a right to emergency medical evaluation. Additionally, this is an opportunity to connect the patient with addiction medicine resources. Refusing transport is inappropriate.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Consent; Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Administer morphine or fentanyl IV to alleviate the withdrawal symptoms
options.explanation: Administering opioid agonists to treat withdrawal in the prehospital setting is outside standard protocols and would reinforce the addiction cycle. Treatment for opioid use disorder requires structured medication-assisted treatment with buprenorphine or methadone in an appropriate clinical setting.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Toxicology and Substance Abuse

questions.clinical_pearl: EMS encounters with patients experiencing opioid withdrawal are critical touchpoints for the healthcare system. Treating these patients with compassion and connecting them to treatment resources can be life-changing. Stigmatizing language and attitudes are barriers to care.

questions.prompt: During transport, the patient becomes tearful and states 'What's the point, I'm just going to use again and die.' How should you respond?

options.text: Take the statement seriously as potential suicidal ideation; ask directly about suicidal thoughts while maintaining a nonjudgmental, empathetic tone
options.explanation: Any statement suggesting hopelessness or death should be taken seriously, especially in the context of substance use disorder and depression. Directly asking about suicidal ideation does not increase risk — it opens communication and allows appropriate risk assessment.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale

options.text: Reassure him that things will get better and change the subject to avoid encouraging suicidal thoughts
options.explanation: Dismissive reassurance invalidates the patient's experience and closes communication. Avoiding the topic does not reduce suicidal risk — it prevents assessment and may leave the patient feeling unheard.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Explain that opioid withdrawal is not fatal and his symptoms will improve in a few days
options.explanation: While medically accurate, this response ignores the patient's emotional distress and potential suicidal ideation. The patient's statement is about hopelessness and fatalism, not a question about withdrawal prognosis.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Document the statement but avoid direct questioning to prevent escalation of negative emotions
options.explanation: Documentation is important, but failing to directly address potential suicidal ideation is a missed opportunity for intervention. Asking about suicidal thoughts does not cause suicidal behavior.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

questions.clinical_pearl: Patients with opioid use disorder have a suicide rate 13 times higher than the general population. Always screen for suicidal ideation during encounters, especially during withdrawal when hopelessness and despair peak.

title: 45F — Threatening Female, Locked in Bathroom
dispatch: 45-year-old female, threatening self-harm, family requesting help

patient.cc: Suicidal threats, self-harm, refusing to come out

scene: You arrive at a suburban home with law enforcement. The patient's husband meets you at the door, visibly distressed. He reports his 45-year-old wife locked herself in the master bathroom 45 minutes ago after a verbal argument. She has been yelling that she wants to die and he heard glass breaking. He states she has a history of bipolar disorder and stopped taking her medications two weeks ago because 'she felt fine.' Through the bathroom door, you can hear the patient alternating between sobbing and shouting. Law enforcement has established verbal contact — she states she has cut her wrists with a broken mirror and 'doesn't want help from anyone.' She refuses to open the door. There is blood visible under the door.

history.pmh: Bipolar I disorder, previous suicide attempt (overdose, 3 years ago), borderline personality disorder
history.meds: Lithium 900 mg daily, quetiapine 200 mg nightly — both discontinued 2 weeks ago

questions.prompt: While law enforcement works to gain access, you assess the situation. Select ALL factors that indicate HIGH risk for completed suicide in this patient.

options.text: Previous suicide attempt by overdose 3 years ago
options.explanation: Prior suicide attempt is the single strongest predictor of future completed suicide. A previous attempt increases lifetime suicide risk by 30-40 times.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; CDC Suicide Risk Factors

options.text: Active self-harm with a method capable of significant blood loss
options.explanation: The patient has already escalated from threats to action by cutting her wrists with broken glass. Active self-harm with a method that can cause life-threatening hemorrhage indicates high immediate risk.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Abrupt discontinuation of psychiatric medications (lithium and quetiapine)
options.explanation: Abrupt discontinuation of mood stabilizers and antipsychotics, particularly lithium, is associated with rapid mood destabilization and increased suicide risk. Lithium itself has anti-suicidal properties, and its discontinuation removes that protective effect.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; APA Practice Guidelines

options.text: Isolation behind a locked door with refusal of help
options.explanation: Self-isolation, barricading, and refusing help indicate intent and planning. The patient has created a barrier to intervention, which increases the lethality of any attempt.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Verbal argument with husband immediately preceding the crisis
options.explanation: While the argument was a precipitating event, interpersonal conflict alone is not a high-risk factor for completed suicide. It is the combination of the argument with the patient's psychiatric history, medication discontinuation, and active self-harm that creates the high-risk profile.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Patient is female and 45 years old
options.explanation: While middle age is a risk period, and females attempt suicide more frequently, gender and age alone are demographic risk factors, not high-risk clinical indicators in an individual assessment.
options.guideline_ref: CDC Suicide Risk Factors

questions.clinical_pearl: Use the mnemonic 'IS PATH WARM' for suicide risk assessment: Ideation, Substance abuse, Purposelessness, Anxiety, Trapped feeling, Hopelessness, Withdrawal, Anger, Recklessness, Mood changes. Multiple positive factors indicate escalating risk.

questions.prompt: Law enforcement has gained entry. The patient is found sitting on the floor with superficial to moderate lacerations on both wrists. She is screaming and swinging the broken mirror shard. Verbal de-escalation has been attempted for 10 minutes without success. She is becoming increasingly agitated. As a Paramedic, what is the MOST appropriate next step?
questions.narrative: Law enforcement has opened the door. The patient has multiple lacerations on both forearms — some are superficial, but two appear deep with steady bleeding. She is holding a large mirror shard and threatening anyone who approaches. She is pale and diaphoretic. Your verbal de-escalation attempts have been unsuccessful — she screams louder when anyone speaks to her.

options.text: Coordinate with law enforcement for physical containment while simultaneously administering ketamine IM for chemical restraint
options.explanation: When verbal de-escalation has failed and the patient poses an imminent danger to herself and others (armed with a sharp weapon, actively bleeding), coordinated physical containment with rapid chemical sedation is appropriate. This allows wound assessment and hemorrhage control. Ketamine IM provides rapid sedation without requiring IV access.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint; National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Continue verbal de-escalation indefinitely because the patient has the right to refuse treatment
options.explanation: While patients have rights, this patient is actively self-harming with signs of hemodynamic compromise (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor). She poses an immediate threat to her own life. Continued delay in treatment when de-escalation has failed for 10+ minutes puts the patient at risk of life-threatening hemorrhage.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; Involuntary Commitment Criteria

options.text: Retreat and wait for a specialized crisis intervention team before taking any action
options.explanation: While crisis teams are valuable, the patient is actively bleeding with signs of hemodynamic instability. Delayed intervention for a specialized team that may take 30+ minutes to arrive is not appropriate when the patient's life is at immediate risk.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Have law enforcement tase the patient to gain immediate control and begin wound care
options.explanation: Conducted electrical weapons in a patient with a sharp object can cause uncontrolled muscle contractions that worsen self-inflicted injuries. Additionally, the patient is in a bathroom with water and hard surfaces, creating additional injury risk. Chemical sedation is the preferred approach.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint

questions.clinical_pearl: Chemical restraint is not punishment — it is a medical intervention to protect the patient. When verbal de-escalation fails and there is imminent danger, timely chemical sedation is more humane and safer than prolonged physical struggle.

questions.prompt: The patient has been sedated and restrained. You can now assess her injuries. Arrange the following interventions in order of priority.
questions.narrative: The patient is now sedated with a GCS of 8. She has two deep lacerations on the left wrist with steady bleeding and multiple superficial lacerations on both forearms. She is pale, diaphoretic, with weak radial pulses.
items: Apply direct pressure with hemostatic dressing to deep wrist lacerations
Secure airway with appropriate adjunct — patient is now obtunded
Establish large-bore IV access and begin rapid fluid resuscitation
Continuous cardiac monitoring and reassess sedation level
Rapid transport to the nearest appropriate facility — notify receiving hospital
questions.explanation: First, secure the airway — with a GCS of 8 post-sedation, the patient cannot reliably protect her own airway. Second, control hemorrhage from the deep lacerations as this is the primary threat. Third, establish IV access and resuscitate — the patient shows signs of hemorrhagic shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor). Fourth, apply monitoring and reassess sedation. Fifth, transport rapidly — this patient needs surgical evaluation, blood products, and psychiatric admission.
questions.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Hemorrhage Control; Behavioral Emergencies; Airway Management
questions.clinical_pearl: Psychiatric patients can have surgical emergencies. Never let the behavioral presentation distract from life-threatening medical or traumatic conditions. Treat the whole patient: stabilize the medical emergency while planning for the psychiatric needs.

questions.prompt: Which legal and documentation considerations are MOST important for this call?

options.text: Document the criteria met for involuntary transport: imminent danger to self, inability to make rational decisions regarding own safety
options.explanation: Involuntary psychiatric transport requires documentation of specific criteria — typically imminent danger to self or others, or grave disability. This patient clearly meets the danger-to-self criterion based on active self-harm with hemodynamic compromise.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Behavioral Emergencies; Involuntary Commitment Laws

options.text: Document the failed verbal de-escalation attempts and the medical necessity for chemical restraint
options.explanation: Chemical restraint documentation should include the attempts at less-restrictive alternatives (verbal de-escalation), the specific threat that necessitated chemical restraint, the medication/dose/route/time, and ongoing reassessment. This protects both the patient and the provider.
options.guideline_ref: NAEMSP Position Statement on Patient Restraint; National EMS Education Standards — Documentation

options.text: Document all injuries with detailed descriptions including location, size, depth, and bleeding characteristics
options.explanation: Detailed wound documentation is essential both for continuity of medical care and for the psychiatric and legal record. Description of self-inflicted injuries may be important for psychiatric disposition and any subsequent legal proceedings.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Documentation; Behavioral Emergencies

options.text: Obtain a signed refusal form from the patient's husband since the patient cannot sign
options.explanation: No refusal is being generated — the patient is being transported involuntarily under emergency commitment criteria. A refusal form is not applicable in this scenario.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Consent and Refusal

options.text: Document the husband's account of events but omit the argument details to protect patient privacy
options.explanation: Precipitating events are clinically relevant and should be documented. The argument is part of the clinical picture that helps receiving providers understand the context of the crisis. Omitting relevant clinical information is not appropriate.
options.guideline_ref: National EMS Education Standards — Documentation

questions.clinical_pearl: Involuntary commitment criteria vary by state but generally include: imminent danger to self, imminent danger to others, or gravely disabled (unable to provide for basic needs). Know your state's specific criteria and required documentation — it is your legal authority to transport without consent.
